were held from March 18–24, 2019, at the [Saitama Super Arena](/wiki/Saitama_Super_Arena "Saitama Super Arena") in [Saitama, Japan](/wiki/Saitama_(city) "Saitama (city)"). Sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU), the [World Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships") are considered the most prestigious event in figure skating. Medals were awarded in [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"). [Nathan Chen](/wiki/Nathan_Chen "Nathan Chen") of the United States won the men's event, [Alina Zagitova](/wiki/Alina_Zagitova "Alina Zagitova") of Russia won the women's event, [Sui Wenjing](/wiki/Sui_Wenjing "Sui Wenjing") and [Han Cong](/wiki/Han_Cong "Han Cong") of China won the pairs event, and [Gabriella Papadakis](/wiki/Gabriella_Papadakis "Gabriella Papadakis") and [Guillaume Cizeron](/wiki/Guillaume_Cizeron "Guillaume Cizeron") of France won the ice dance event. The competition would have determined the entry quotas for each skating federation at the [2020 World Championships](/wiki/2020_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2020 World Figure Skating Championships"); however, the 2020 World Champions were eventually cancelled due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").

The **2019 World Figure Skating Championships** were held from March 18–24, 2019, at the [Saitama Super Arena](/wiki/Saitama_Super_Arena "Saitama Super Arena") in [Saitama, Japan](/wiki/Saitama_(city) "Saitama (city)"). Sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU), the [World Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships") are considered the most prestigious event in figure skating. Medals were awarded in [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"). [Nathan Chen](/wiki/Nathan_Chen "Nathan Chen") of the United States won the men's event, [Alina Zagitova](/wiki/Alina_Zagitova "Alina Zagitova") of Russia won the women's event, [Sui Wenjing](/wiki/Sui_Wenjing "Sui Wenjing") and [Han Cong](/wiki/Han_Cong "Han Cong") of China won the pairs event, and [Gabriella Papadakis](/wiki/Gabriella_Papadakis "Gabriella Papadakis") and [Guillaume Cizeron](/wiki/Guillaume_Cizeron "Guillaume Cizeron") of France won the ice dance event. The competition would have determined the entry quotas for each skating federation at the [2020 World Championships](/wiki/2020_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2020 World Figure Skating Championships"); however, the 2020 World Champions were eventually cancelled due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

The [World Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships") are considered the most prestigious event in figure skating.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The 2019 World Figure Skating Championships were held from March 18–24, 2019, at the [Saitama Super Arena](/wiki/Saitama_Super_Arena "Saitama Super Arena") in [Saitama, Japan](/wiki/Saitama_(city) "Saitama (city)").[[2]](#cite_note-Announcement-2)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Qualification")]

The number of entries from each nation for the 2019 World Championships was based on the results of the [2018 World Championships](/wiki/2018_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2018 World Figure Skating Championships"). These nations were eligible to enter more than one skater or team in the indicated disciplines.

## Results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Results")]

### Men's singles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Men's singles")]

Defending World Champion [Nathan Chen](/wiki/Nathan_Chen "Nathan Chen") finished first after the short program, with his American teammate [Jason Brown](/wiki/Jason_Brown_(figure_skater) "Jason Brown (figure skater)") finishing in second place. Chen was satisfied with his performance, saying, "There are always things I can do better, things I can improve on but ultimately everything I did, I did as best as I could and I hope to continue that into the free program".[[15]](#cite_note-PairsandMensCBC-15) Brown's short program, despite not having any quad jumps in comparison to his competitors, has been praised as one of the best short programs of the season.[[16]](#cite_note-WhatIRemember-16) After moving to Toronto in the off-season, Brown reflected on his newfound maturity, noting, "There's a bit of confidence, a maturity confidence. As far as the skating goes, there's still a lot of changes that we have to make, but as the season's gone on, I've gotten a lot more confidence. I think it's a sense of maturity, the way that I'm carrying myself."[[17]](#cite_note-DayFourOlympic-17) Two-time Olympic and World Champion [Yuzuru Hanyu](/wiki/Yuzuru_Hanyu "Yuzuru Hanyu"), rounded out the top 3 despite doubling his quad [salchow](/wiki/Salchow_jump "Salchow jump") attempt. While commenting about his performance at the press conference Hanyu expressed his surprise of being awarded third place, despite missing the second half of the season due to an injured ankle, "I'm disappointed with my short program, I made a big mistake and I've got to reform and improve for the free skate, but I'm honored to be in the top three."[[15]](#cite_note-PairsandMensCBC-15) [Four Continents Bronze Medallist](/wiki/2019_Four_Continents_Figure_Skating_Championships "2019 Four Continents Figure Skating Championships") [Vincent Zhou](/wiki/Vincent_Zhou "Vincent Zhou") finished in fourth, [European bronze medallist](/wiki/2019_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2019 European Figure Skating Championships") [Matteo Rizzo](/wiki/Matteo_Rizzo "Matteo Rizzo") was fifth, while Four Continents champion, [Olympic silver medallist](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_singles "Figure skating at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Men's singles") and two-time defending silver medallist [Shoma Uno](/wiki/Shoma_Uno "Shoma Uno") completed the top six.

Chen maintained the lead he gained in the short program, becoming the first American male skater to defend a World Title since [Scott Hamilton](/wiki/Scott_Hamilton_(figure_skater) "Scott Hamilton (figure skater)") in [1984](/wiki/1984_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1984 World Figure Skating Championships").[[18]](#cite_note-MensandIceDanceResults-18) He finished 22 points ahead of Hanyu, who became the first skater to break the 200-point mark since the introduction of the +5 GOE system, marking the fifth consecutive year of a Japanese skater winning the silver medal.[[18]](#cite_note-MensandIceDanceResults-18) Hanyu was called for an under-rotation and uneasy landing on his quad [salchow](/wiki/Salchow_jump "Salchow jump"), missed half of the season due to a lingering ankle injury and was proud of his performance despite not having enough strength in his ankle.[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19)[[20]](#cite_note-MensIceDanceOlympic-20) Zhou's bronze medal was his first major ISU Championship senior medal, making himself and Chen the first two American men to make the world championship podium since [1996](/wiki/1996_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1996 World Figure Skating Championships"). After breaking the [free skate](/wiki/Free_skating "Free skating") world record at [Four Continents](/wiki/2019_Four_Continents_Figure_Skating_Championships "2019 Four Continents Figure Skating Championships"), Uno's fourth-place finish was a shock to the home crowd—with a fall on his quad flip, an under-rotation on a combination jump and incomplete landing call on his opening quad salchow—was notably in tears when speaking to Japanese media stating feelings of regret and disappointment.[[20]](#cite_note-MensIceDanceOlympic-20) When reflecting on his season, Uno was upset at his performance "If I recall, there are more competitions that I got disappointed over joyful ones in this season, [...] Overall, I am still disappointed in myself. I need to become much stronger mentally."[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19) [Jin Boyang](/wiki/Jin_Boyang "Jin Boyang") finished in fifth with two triple Axels and a big cheer from the crowd, with his performance component score bringing down his marks.[[20]](#cite_note-MensIceDanceOlympic-20) Despite a second-place finish in the short program, Brown could not maintain his lead and dropped to ninth overall after a messy free skate, including a fall on his quad salchow, although was positive about his incredible improvement throughout the season.[[18]](#cite_note-MensandIceDanceResults-18)[[20]](#cite_note-MensIceDanceOlympic-20) [Kevin Aymoz](/wiki/Kevin_Aymoz "Kevin Aymoz") suffered an injury to his hands after cutting them when trying to save a jump gone wrong, resulting in blood dripping on the ice.

### Women's singles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Women's singles")]

[2018 Olympic gold medallist](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Ladies%27_singles "Figure skating at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Ladies' singles") [Alina Zagitova](/wiki/Alina_Zagitova "Alina Zagitova") took the lead in the short program, ahead of home crowd favourite and Japanese national champion [Kaori Sakamoto](/wiki/Kaori_Sakamoto "Kaori Sakamoto") who achieved a personal best score after performing a near perfect triple [flip](/wiki/Flip_jump "Flip jump")-triple [toe-loop](/wiki/Toe_loop_jump "Toe loop jump") combination.[[21]](#cite_note-PairsandWomenCBC-21)[[22]](#cite_note-LadiesPairsOlympic-22) Zagitova mentioned that she was nervous before beginning her program as she had experienced several mistakes and errors throughout the season, although stated that she felt more mentally prepared in comparison to the rest of her season.[[23]](#cite_note-LadiesPairsBleacher-23) Sakamoto was pleased with her performance, "I was able to execute everything as I did in practice and will aim to do that again in the free skate."[[23]](#cite_note-LadiesPairsBleacher-23) Zagitova's training partner [Elizabet Tursynbaeva](/wiki/Elizabet_Tursynbaeva "Elizabet Tursynbaeva") surprised both critics and the crowd to finish in third. After a rocky season, two-time world champion [Evgenia Medvedeva](/wiki/Evgenia_Medvedeva "Evgenia Medvedeva") finished fourth by achieving a seasons best for a short program that was first performed in December, receiving a standing ovation from the Japanese crowd despite an under-rotation call on her triple toe-loop.[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19)[[22]](#cite_note-LadiesPairsOlympic-22) Short program world record holder and [Grand Prix Final](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "2018–19 Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final") champion, [Rika Kihira](/wiki/Rika_Kihira "Rika Kihira") finished in seventh after singling her triple Axel attempt.[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19)[[21]](#cite_note-PairsandWomenCBC-21)[[22]](#cite_note-LadiesPairsOlympic-22) [2017 World bronze medallist](/wiki/2017_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2017 World Figure Skating Championships") and [2018 Olympic Team Champion](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Team_event "Figure skating at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Team event") [Gabrielle Daleman](/wiki/Gabrielle_Daleman "Gabrielle Daleman") made her return to international competition after taking personal leave for mental health reasons, finishing in 11th despite skating clean.[[21]](#cite_note-PairsandWomenCBC-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/World_Championships_2019_-_Ladies_%28Podium%29.jpg/250px-World_Championships_2019_-_Ladies_%28Podium%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:World_Championships_2019_-_Ladies_(Podium).jpg)

Ladies' podium *(left to right)* – Tursynbaeva (silver), Zagitova (gold) and Medvedeva (bronze)

Zagitova took first in the free skate to win her first world title, becoming the first female skater since [Yuna Kim](/wiki/Yuna_Kim "Yuna Kim") to complete a Career Grand Slam after silver medal finishes at the [Grand Prix Final](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "2018–19 Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final") and [Europeans](/wiki/2019_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2019 European Figure Skating Championships").[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19) When speaking to the media Zagitova stated "I realized that I needed a clean skate and I went into each jump determined to land it".[[24]](#cite_note-LadiesandIceDanceCanada-24) Tursynbaeva became the first senior woman to land a quad jump, completing a clean quad salchow in the free skate to take silver for Kazakhstan, their first in any discipline since [Denis Ten](/wiki/Denis_Ten "Denis Ten") in [2015](/wiki/2015_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2015 World Figure Skating Championships"). Tursynbaeva couldn't believe she landed her quad after falling in her previous two attempts in competition.[[24]](#cite_note-LadiesandIceDanceCanada-24) Medvedeva narrowly beat Kihira for the bronze by a tenth of a mark, despite Kihira beating Medvedeva in the free skate the deficit from the short program it was not enough to change the standings. Kihira's Japanese teammates, short program silver medallist Sakamoto and [Satoko Miyahara](/wiki/Satoko_Miyahara "Satoko Miyahara") finished fifth and sixth, respectively, with Sakamoto popping her planned triple-flip which cost her a place on the podium.[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19)[[25]](#cite_note-LadiesIceDanceOlympic-25) The judging panel came under scrutiny with Medvedeva not being called for her lutz performed with the wrong edge, with critics and fans criticising a lack of consistency amongst the judging of other skaters, marring the final result.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Pairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Pairs")]

[Evgenia Tarasova](/wiki/Evgenia_Tarasova "Evgenia Tarasova") & [Vladimir Morozov](/wiki/Vladimir_Morozov_(figure_skater) "Vladimir Morozov (figure skater)") finished the short program by breaking the world record, competing with their [2018 Olympic short program](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Pair_skating "Figure skating at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Pair skating").[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19) In their second competition of the season, 2017 World Champions [Sui Wenjing](/wiki/Sui_Wenjing "Sui Wenjing") & [Han Cong](/wiki/Han_Cong "Han Cong") finished in second place, with their training partners and compatriots [Peng Cheng](/wiki/Peng_Cheng "Peng Cheng") & [Jin Yang](/wiki/Jin_Yang "Jin Yang") finishing in third. Event favourites [Vanessa James](/wiki/Vanessa_James "Vanessa James") & [Morgan Cipres](/wiki/Morgan_Cipres "Morgan Cipres") struggled with the short program once again, after James accidentally collided with [Matteo Guarise](/wiki/Matteo_Guarise "Matteo Guarise") in the warm-up that left both athletes shaken.[[16]](#cite_note-WhatIRemember-16)[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19) Guarise was left with bruises on his left hip and pain in his left knee, "I wanted to slow down when I saw Vanessa, I am a big guy and she is so tiny. I didn't want to hurt her."[[27]](#cite_note-27) James, who was left uninjured, when discussing the incident with Olympic Channel she noted, "we've had some hard situations but we always push through them."[[28]](#cite_note-28)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Sui_Wenjing_and_Han_Cong_at_the_World_Championships_2019_-_Awarding_ceremony.jpg/250px-Sui_Wenjing_and_Han_Cong_at_the_World_Championships_2019_-_Awarding_ceremony.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sui_Wenjing_and_Han_Cong_at_the_World_Championships_2019_-_Awarding_ceremony.jpg)

World Champions, Sui Wenjing and Han Cong (China), with their gold medals after the pairs event podium presentation

Sui and Han broke the free skate world record to win their second world title in three years, with the skates by the top four considered to be one of the best in recent world championships, with Sui and Han's considered one of the best pairs free skates of all time.[[17]](#cite_note-DayFourOlympic-17)[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19) Sui discussed the difficulties and challenges they had throughout the season while recovering from ankle surgery, Sui noted to the media "In the short, we performed very well, and as the last to go on today, we knew that if we did well and challenged ourselves, we would win."[[17]](#cite_note-DayFourOlympic-17) Although Morozov stumbled his side-by-side jump, he and his partner Tarasova finished in second place, with their compatriots [Natalia Zabiiako](/wiki/Natalia_Zabiiako "Natalia Zabiiako") and [Alexander Enbert](/wiki/Alexander_Enbert "Alexander Enbert") taking a surprising bronze medal after finishing in fourth in the short program.[[17]](#cite_note-DayFourOlympic-17) Despite finishing third in the free skate and fifth overall, James and Cipres were frustrated with their performances, with Cipres adding although they were proud of their performance, "we want this title, everybody knows that we can do it, we know that we can do it, and we are going to do it."[[17]](#cite_note-DayFourOlympic-17)

### Ice dance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ice dance")]

Defending world champions [Gabriella Papadakis](/wiki/Gabriella_Papadakis "Gabriella Papadakis") & [Guillaume Cizeron](/wiki/Guillaume_Cizeron "Guillaume Cizeron") completed the Rhythm Dance with a five-point lead over the Russian team of [Victoria Sinitsina](/wiki/Victoria_Sinitsina "Victoria Sinitsina") & [Nikita Katsalapov](/wiki/Nikita_Katsalapov "Nikita Katsalapov"). Papadakis stated that an improvement of their technique training helped them gain the points advantage they wanted going into the free skate, calling their skate "the best performance".[[25]](#cite_note-LadiesIceDanceOlympic-25) [Olympic Ice Dance Champion](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Ice_dance "Figure skating at the 2014 Winter Olympics – Ice dance") [Meryl Davis](/wiki/Meryl_Davis "Meryl Davis"), praised Papadakis and Cizeron for their performance, calling it "very special, [...] remarkable" and "in a class of their own".[[25]](#cite_note-LadiesIceDanceOlympic-25) [Alexandra Stepanova](/wiki/Alexandra_Stepanova "Alexandra Stepanova") & [Ivan Bukin](/wiki/Ivan_Bukin "Ivan Bukin") finished in third, leading by 0.01 over Americans and defending silver medallists [Madison Hubbell](/wiki/Madison_Hubbell "Madison Hubbell") & [Zachary Donohue](/wiki/Zachary_Donohue "Zachary Donohue"). Hubbell was positive about their performance as they had improved their technical score, which was their main goal for their performance.[[25]](#cite_note-LadiesIceDanceOlympic-25) The reigning bronze medallists, [Kaitlyn Weaver](/wiki/Kaitlyn_Weaver "Kaitlyn Weaver") & [Andrew Poje](/wiki/Andrew_Poje "Andrew Poje") finished in fifth, with Weaver calling their performance "a magical experience [...] but not perfect".[[24]](#cite_note-LadiesandIceDanceCanada-24) [Laurence Fournier Beaudry](/wiki/Laurence_Fournier_Beaudry "Laurence Fournier Beaudry") & [Nikolaj Sorensen](/wiki/Nikolaj_Sorensen "Nikolaj Sorensen") made their first appearance for Canada after competing for Denmark in previous championships, with Sorensen suffering a wardrobe malfunction that placed them in tenth.[[24]](#cite_note-LadiesandIceDanceCanada-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Victoria_Sinitsina_and_Nikita_Katsalapov_-_2019_World_Championships.jpg/250px-Victoria_Sinitsina_and_Nikita_Katsalapov_-_2019_World_Championships.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoria_Sinitsina_and_Nikita_Katsalapov_-_2019_World_Championships.jpg)

Victoria Sinitsina and Nikita Katsalapov at the conclusion of their silver medal-winning free skate

Papadakis and Cizeron maintained their lead from the Rhythm Dance winning their fourth World Title in five years by finishing eleven points higher overall than silver medallists Sinitsina and Katsalapov, with the French team's only loss being to [Tessa Virtue](/wiki/Tessa_Virtue "Tessa Virtue") & [Scott Moir](/wiki/Scott_Moir "Scott Moir") in [2017](/wiki/2017_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2017 World Figure Skating Championships").[[18]](#cite_note-MensandIceDanceResults-18) Cizeron was happy with their performance, while Papadakis reflected at the last time the world championships were in Saitama, "we were exactly here five years ago for the worlds in Saitama and it's funny to remember the whole experience we came in this five years, where we were at the time and where we are now".[[20]](#cite_note-MensIceDanceOlympic-20) Katsalapov achieved his first major ISU Championship medal since his bronze medal at the [Sochi Olympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics") with previous partner [Elena Ilinykh](/wiki/Elena_Ilinykh "Elena Ilinykh"), while it was current partner Sinitsina first overall. Sinitsina and Katsalapov were also the first Russian Ice Dance team to finish on the worlds podium since [Ekaterina Bobrova](/wiki/Ekaterina_Bobrova "Ekaterina Bobrova") & [Dmitri Soloviev](/wiki/Dmitri_Soloviev "Dmitri Soloviev") in [2013](/wiki/2013_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2013 World Figure Skating Championships").[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19) Hubbell and Donohue took the bronze ahead of Stepanova and Bukin, making it the fifth consecutive year of an American Ice Dance team finishing on the podium.[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19) Hubbell explained how their partnership had improved throughout the year and the risk to change their program choreography weeks before the world championship, "to be able to skate an emotionally strong performance as well as a technically strong performance, it's what every athlete wants."[[20]](#cite_note-MensIceDanceOlympic-20) Despite receiving a standing ovation from the crowd, Stepanova and Bukin's technical content was not enough to push them into the top three, missing by two points.[[20]](#cite_note-MensIceDanceOlympic-20) Weaver and Poje completed the top five, despite missing the first half of the season, performing a tribute program to late skater [Denis Ten](/wiki/Denis_Ten "Denis Ten").[[19]](#cite_note-TopMoments-19)[[20]](#cite_note-MensIceDanceOlympic-20)

## Controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Controversy")]

### Mariah Bell/Lim Eun-soo collision

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Mariah Bell/Lim Eun-soo collision")]

During a practice session run-through of American skater [Mariah Bell](/wiki/Mariah_Bell "Mariah Bell")'s short program, Bell skated past Korean skater [Lim Eun-soo](/wiki/Lim_Eun-soo "Lim Eun-soo"), who was along the baseboards. As Bell approached, her toe-pick scraped the back of Lim's left calf, causing a minor injury. Bell had right-of-way on the ice at the time due to it being her program run-through. Lim's management agency, [All That Skate](/wiki/All_That_Skate "All That Skate"), claimed that the incident was a deliberate attack by Bell on her training teammate in [Rafael Arutyunyan](/wiki/Rafael_Arutyunyan "Rafael Arutyunyan")'s coaching group, while Bell said it was an accident. Because Bell's music was playing during practice, she had the right-of-way at the time and Lim should have been aware of where Bell was on the ice.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) ISU released a statement clearing Bell of wrongdoing in the incident.[[31]](#cite_note-bell-lim-31) [U.S. Figure Skating](/wiki/U.S._Figure_Skating "U.S. Figure Skating") extended an apology to the Korean Skating Union over the incident.[[31]](#cite_note-bell-lim-31)
